summary: O'Neil shares outrage over a Barbie book called 'I Can Be a Computer Engineer' that perpetuates sexist stereotypes by having Barbie claim she only does design work and needs boys to do the actual programming. The post highlights blogger Pamela Ribon's viral takedown of the book, which exposes how even supposedly empowering children's media undermines girls' confidence in technical fields. O'Neil amplifies the criticism by sharing the original post and pointing to satirical Amazon reviews and a feminist remix of the book. The brief post serves as a signal boost for community outrage over casual sexism in STEM messaging to children.
key_points: A Barbie book marketed as empowering ('I Can Be a Computer Engineer') actually shows Barbie needing boys to do the real coding work
The post amplifies Pamela Ribon's viral critique that exposed the book's sexist messaging
Community response included satirical Amazon reviews and a feminist remix showing Barbie actually coding
Even well-intentioned attempts at representation can reinforce harmful stereotypes about women in tech
money_quotes: "I'm only creating the design ideas," Barbie says, laughing. "I'll need Steven and Brian's help to turn it into a real game!"
key_insight: Children's media claiming to empower girls in STEM can actually reinforce the very stereotypes it pretends to challenge.
